title: Reports & Audit
intro: This screen has two jobs. On the left you generate exports: data files that pull together accounting, tax, regulatory, and day-to-day operational records into a single download. On the right you search the audit log: a permanent record of who did what, to what, and when.

A "report" here is just a structured snapshot of platform data for a chosen date range, produced as a file you can hand to accountants, auditors, regulators, or banks. You pick what data you want (the report type), the file format (CSV, PDF, or ZIP), how much sensitive detail to include (the redaction mode), and the time period. The system builds the file on demand and gives it back to you to download.

The audit log is the platform's tamper-proof history book. Every meaningful action by staff or the system is written here once and can never be edited or deleted (it is "append-only"). It records the actor (who), the action (what they did), the target (what it was done to), and the exact time. It is your evidence trail when someone asks "who changed this?".

Important context for a newcomer: PII means "personally identifiable information" such as names and account-owner IDs. Some reports and the "Full" redaction mode expose this sensitive data, so the system restricts them to senior staff (superadmins). Generating any report itself writes an entry into the audit log, so report generation is never invisible.
whatYouDoHere: Generate an accounting, tax, regulatory, or operational export by choosing a report type, output format, redaction mode, and date range, then clicking Generate report.
Choose how much sensitive detail the export contains using the redaction mode (Redacted hides personal identifiers; Full shows everything and is superadmin-only).
Set the reporting period either with a quick preset (daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, yearly, calendar year) or with custom start and end dates.
Download the finished file, and verify its integrity using the SHA-256 checksum the system records for each run.
Search and review the immutable audit log to see who performed which action against which target, and exactly when it happened.

figures.caption: The Report generation form: choose what to export, in what format, how redacted, and for which period.
figures.summary: A form that builds a data export on demand. You select the report type, output format, redaction mode, and period, then click Generate report; the system returns a downloadable file plus a checksum and manifest.

annotations.label: Report generation (panel title)
annotations.text: Marks the left-hand panel where you build exports. It generates accounting, tax, regulatory, and operational files on demand for a chosen date range.

annotations.label: Report type (field)
annotations.text: Picks which dataset to export. This is the single most important choice: it decides what rows appear in the file.

annotations.label: Report type dropdown
annotations.text: The list of available exports. The options are: Operational subledger, Trial balance, Garanta accrued revenue, Bexio accounting export, Bank operations, Reconciliation, Investor balances, Balance ageing, Withdrawals, Loan funding, Repayment status, Default exposure, Recovery/write-off, FX activity, KYC status, Audit log, Failed outbox, Participant account statement, and Annual tax information. Gotcha: four of these contain restricted data and can only be generated by a superadmin: KYC status, Audit log, Participant account statement, and Annual tax information. A regular admin who selects one of these gets a permission error ("requires an active superadmin").

annotations.label: Output format (field)
annotations.text: Chooses the file type the export is delivered as. It does not change which data is included, only how the file is packaged.

annotations.label: Output format dropdown
annotations.text: Three choices: CSV (a plain spreadsheet-style text file, best for accountants and re-import), PDF (a formatted, print-ready document), and ZIP (an evidence package that bundles the data and a manifest). CSV comes back as plain text; PDF and ZIP come back base64-encoded, which means you cannot read them in the on-screen preview and must click Download artifact to save the actual file.

annotations.label: Redaction mode (field)
annotations.text: Controls how much personally identifiable information (PII) the export reveals. This is a privacy and compliance control, so treat it carefully.

annotations.label: Redaction mode dropdown
annotations.text: Two choices. Redacted (the default and safe option): personal identifiers such as account-owner IDs, lender user IDs, bank references, and evidence references are replaced with the literal word REDACTED. Full: every identifier and detail is shown in the clear. Hard rule: Full mode is superadmin-only. If a regular admin selects Full, the system refuses with "Full report exports require an active superadmin." Use Redacted unless you have a specific, authorized reason for Full.

annotations.label: Period preset (field)
annotations.text: Lets you pick a standard reporting window quickly instead of typing two dates.

annotations.label: Period preset dropdown
annotations.text: Choices: Custom, Daily, Weekly, Monthly, Quarterly, Yearly, and Calendar year. With Custom, you must fill in both Start date and End date below. With any other preset, the system ignores the start date and uses the End / anchor date as the anchor point, then expands it automatically: Daily = that single day; Weekly = the Monday-to-Sunday week containing it; Monthly = the whole calendar month; Quarterly = the whole quarter; Yearly and Calendar year = the whole calendar year (Jan 1 to Dec 31).

annotations.label: Start date (field)
annotations.text: The first day included in the export. It is only used when Period preset is set to Custom. With any other preset this field is ignored. Rule: in Custom mode the end date must be on or after the start date, or the request is rejected.

annotations.label: End / anchor date (field)
annotations.text: Does double duty. In Custom mode it is the last day included. With any other preset it is the "anchor" date the system builds the window around (for example, in Monthly mode the system takes the month that contains this date). It is required for the preset modes.

annotations.label: Destination note (field)
annotations.text: A free-text note describing why or for whom the report was produced (for example "Q2 auditor request"). It is stored on the report run record and the audit log records whether a note was present, so use it to leave a paper trail. It does not change the report's contents.

annotations.label: Generate report (button)
annotations.text: Builds the export now. On success the system creates a permanent report-run record, writes an audit-log entry (action reporting.report_generated), and returns the file plus its details. The result panel that appears below shows the filename, the row count, a SHA-256 checksum you can use to prove the file was not altered, the generation timestamp, the encoding, a content preview (CSV only), and a manifest. A Download artifact button there saves the actual file. Gotcha: in preview mode no real file is produced; only a placeholder message appears.

notes: Permission gates enforced by the backend: only an active admin can generate any report; Full redaction mode is superadmin-only; and the four restricted types (KYC status, Audit log, Participant account statement, Annual tax information) are superadmin-only regardless of redaction mode.
Every generated report is itself recorded: it creates an immutable report-run row, a report event, and an audit-log entry. There is no way to generate a report silently.
Each report run carries a SHA-256 checksum and a fixed definition version (reporting-v2). The checksum lets a recipient confirm the file they received is byte-for-byte the one that was generated.
The generated filename follows the pattern reporttype_startdate_enddate.extension (for example operational_subledger_2026-01-01_2026-03-31.csv).
PDF and ZIP outputs are base64-encoded and cannot be read in the on-screen preview; you must download them. Only CSV shows a readable preview (first 2000 characters).

figures.caption: The Audit event search panel: a read-only, time-ordered table of recent platform actions.
figures.summary: A table of the most recent audit-log entries (newest first). Each row is one recorded action and shows when it happened, what it was, who did it, and what it was done to. The log is append-only evidence: entries can never be edited or removed.

annotations.label: Audit event search (panel title)
annotations.text: Marks the right-hand panel that lists recent platform actions for operational review. It is a read-only view onto the immutable audit log, not a place to change anything.

annotations.label: Refresh (button)
annotations.text: Reloads the latest audit entries from the backend. Use it after performing actions elsewhere to pull in newly recorded events. It only re-reads data; it cannot alter the log.

annotations.label: When (column)
annotations.text: The exact date and time the action was recorded (the occurred_at timestamp). Rows are shown newest first. This timestamp is set automatically when the event is written and cannot be changed afterwards.

annotations.label: Action (column)
annotations.text: What happened, as a machine-readable code (for example reporting.report_generated). The prefix before the dot tells you the area of the platform; the part after describes the act. This is how you find all events of a given kind.

annotations.label: Actor (column)
annotations.text: Who performed the action, shown as actor_type:actor_id (for example admin:<id>). The actor can be a staff member or an automated system process. This answers "who did it?"

annotations.label: Target (column)
annotations.text: What the action was performed on, shown as target_type:target_id (for example report_run:<id>). Together with Action and Actor it forms the full sentence: this actor did this action to this target at this time.

notes: The audit log is append-only: entries are written once and the system actively blocks any attempt to update or delete them. This is what makes it trustworthy evidence in a dispute or audit.
This panel loads the most recent entries (up to 100) and shows them newest-first. It is a review view; there are no editable filter inputs on this figure, so use Refresh to pull the latest events.
Beyond the four visible columns, each event also stores a request ID and a free-form metadata object behind the scenes, which give extra context for investigations.
If no events are returned the panel shows an empty state reminding you that richer filtering becomes available once the production audit-log endpoint is fully live.
Note the distinction: the Audit log report type on the left exports the audit log as a file (and is superadmin-only), while this panel is for quick on-screen review.
